docs.title: Kauffman's NK Landscape Model
docs.text:
class NK(n, k, exponent=1.0, seed=None)

Class implementing the Kauffman's NK landscape model.

The Kauffman NK model is a tunable fitness landscape model where \(N\) genes or loci interact with \(K\) other randomly chosen loci. This model is notable for its ability to interpolate between simple, smooth landscapes and complex, rugged ones. When \(K=0\), each locus contributes to fitness independently, resulting in an additive model (a smooth landscape). As \(K\) increases, the number of epistatic interactions grows. When \(K=N-1\) (where \(N\) is the number of variables n), every locus interacts with all other loci, leading to a highly rugged landscape that approaches the characteristics of a House-of-Cards (HoC) model.

Parameters:

  • n (int): The number of variables (genes) in the problem.
  • k (int): The number of interacting components (genes) for each variable. Must be non-negative and less than n.
  • exponent (float, default: 1.0): An exponent used to transform the final (normalized) fitness value.
  • seed (int | None, optional): Seed for the random number generator to ensure reproducibility.

Methods:

docs.title: get_all_configs()
docs.text:
Generates all \(2^n\) possible binary configurations for the NK model.

  • Returns:
    • iterator: An iterator yielding all binary configurations (tuples of 0s and 1s) of length n.

docs.title: evaluate(config)
docs.text:
Calculates the fitness of a given binary configuration.

The fitness is determined by summing the contributions of each of the \(N\) components. Each component's contribution is a random value (generated once and stored) determined by its state and the states of its \(K\) interacting neighbors. The sum of contributions is then normalized by \(N\) and raised to the power of exponent.

  • Parameters:
    • config (tuple | list): A binary configuration (e.g., (0, 1, 0, ..., 1)) of length n.
  • Returns:
    • float: The calculated fitness value for the configuration.
  • Raises:
    • ValueError: If the length of config does not match n.

docs.title: RoughMountFuji
docs.text:
class RoughMountFuji(n, alpha=0.5, seed=None)

The Rough Mount Fuji (RMF) model describes a fitness landscape that interpolates between a perfectly smooth, additive landscape and a completely rugged, random landscape. It achieves this by combining two components: 1. An additive (smooth) component: Each locus makes an independent contribution to fitness if its state is '1'. In this implementation, these potential contributions (smooth_contribution) are drawn uniformly from [-1.0, 1.0] for each locus during initialization. The total smooth value for a configuration is the sum of contributions from loci with value '1'. 2. A random (rugged) component: Each genotype (configuration) is assigned an independent random fitness value, similar to the House-of-Cards model. In this implementation, this rugged_value is drawn uniformly from [0,1) when the configuration is first evaluated.

The parameter \(\alpha\) (alpha) controls the balance between these two components. The fitness is calculated as: \(Fitness = (1 - \alpha) \times \text{smooth\_value} + \alpha \times \text{rugged\_value}\) When \(\alpha = 0\), the landscape is purely additive (smooth). When \(\alpha = 1\), the landscape is purely random (rugged), resembling the HoC model. Values of \(\alpha\) between 0 and 1 create landscapes with varying degrees of ruggedness superimposed on a general trend.

Parameters:

  • n (int): The number of variables in the optimization problem.
  • alpha (float, default: 0.5): The ruggedness parameter, controlling the balance between the smooth and rugged components. Must be between 0.0 (smoothest) and 1.0 (most rugged).
  • seed (int | None, optional): Seed for the random number generator.

Methods:

docs.title: get_all_configs()
docs.text:
Generates all \(2^n\) possible binary configurations for the RMF model.

  • Returns:
    • iterator: An iterator yielding all binary configurations (tuples of 0s and 1s) of length n.

docs.title: evaluate(config)
docs.text:
Calculates the fitness of a given binary configuration.

The fitness is computed as a weighted sum of the smooth component (sum of smooth_contribution[i] * config[i]) and the rugged component (a random value unique to the config, generated on first encounter).

  • Parameters:
    • config (tuple | list): A binary configuration (e.g., (0, 1, 0, ..., 1)) of length n.
  • Returns:
    • float: The calculated fitness value for the configuration.
  • Raises:
    • ValueError: If the length of config does not match n.

docs.title: HoC
docs.text:
class HoC(n, seed=None)

The House of Cards (HoC) model represents a maximally rugged and uncorrelated fitness landscape. In this model, the fitness of each genotype (configuration) is an independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) random variable. There are no correlations between the fitness values of neighboring genotypes, implying complete epistatic interactions across all loci.

This implementation defines the HoC model as a special case of the RoughMountFuji model where the ruggedness parameter \(\alpha\) is set to 1.0. This means the fitness of a configuration is solely determined by its random (rugged) component. Each unique configuration is assigned a random fitness value, drawn uniformly from [0,1) using the instance's random number generator, upon its first evaluation.

Parameters:

  • n (int): The number of variables in the optimization problem.
  • seed (int | None, optional): Seed for the random number generator.

Methods:

(Inherits get_all_configs() from RoughMountFuji)

docs.title: evaluate(config)
docs.text:
Calculates the fitness of a given binary configuration.

For the HoC model, the fitness is a purely random value assigned to each unique configuration. If the configuration has been evaluated before, its stored random fitness is returned; otherwise, a new random fitness (uniformly from [0,1)) is generated using the instance's seeded RNG and stored.

  • Parameters:
    • config (tuple | list): A binary configuration (e.g., (0, 1, 0, ..., 1)) of length n.
  • Returns:
    • float: The random fitness value assigned to the configuration.
  • Raises:
    • ValueError: If the length of config does not match n.

docs.title: Additive
docs.text:
class Additive(n, seed=None)

The Additive model describes a fitness landscape where there are no epistatic interactions between loci. The fitness of a genotype is simply the sum of independent contributions from each locus. The contribution of each locus depends only on its own allelic state (0 or 1) and is independent of the states of other loci. This results in a smooth, often unimodal, landscape.

In this implementation, for each of the \(N\) loci, two random values are generated during initialization (uniformly from [0,1) using the instance's RNG): one for the contribution if the allele at that locus is 0, and one if the allele is 1. The total fitness of a configuration is the sum of these pre-assigned contributions based on the alleles present in the configuration.

Parameters:

  • n (int): The number of variables in the optimization problem.
  • seed (int | None, optional): Seed for the random number generator.

Methods:

docs.title: get_all_configs()
docs.text:
Generates all \(2^n\) possible binary configurations for the Additive model.

  • Returns:
    • iterator: An iterator yielding all binary configurations (tuples of 0s and 1s) of length n.

docs.title: evaluate(config)
docs.text:
Calculates the fitness of a given binary configuration.

The fitness is computed by summing the independent, pre-defined contributions for the allele (0 or 1) at each locus in the configuration.

  • Parameters:
    • config (tuple | list): A binary configuration (e.g., (0, 1, 0, ..., 1)) of length n.
  • Returns:
    • float: The calculated fitness value for the configuration.
  • Raises:
    • ValueError: If the length of config does not match n.

docs.title: Eggbox
docs.text:
class Eggbox(n, frequency=1.0, seed=None)

The Eggbox model represents a deterministic fitness landscape characterized by regularly spaced peaks and valleys, analogous to the structure of an egg carton. Unlike models incorporating stochasticity in fitness assignment per se, the Eggbox landscape's fitness values are defined by a mathematical function. Specifically, the fitness of a configuration depends solely on the sum of its elements (i.e., the number of '1's in a binary string).

The landscape is generated using a squared sine function: \(Fitness = \sin^2(\text{frequency} \cdot (\sum config_i) \cdot \pi)\). Here, \(\sum config_i\) is the sum of the elements in the configuration (number of 1s). This function creates a periodic pattern of fitness values (ranging from 0 to 1) as the sum ranges from 0 to \(N\). The frequency parameter controls the density of peaks and valleys. This model can exhibit strong epistasis and multiple local optima due to its oscillatory nature. The seed parameter is present for API consistency but does not affect landscape generation, as it is entirely deterministic.

Parameters:

  • n (int): The number of variables in the optimization problem.
  • frequency (float, default: 1.0): The frequency of the peaks and troughs in the landscape. Higher values lead to more oscillations within the range of possible sums (0 to n). Must be positive.
  • seed (int | None, optional): Seed for the random number generator. (Note: This problem is deterministic; the seed is included for API consistency with the base class and has no effect on the landscape generation).

Methods:

docs.title: get_all_configs()
docs.text:
Generates all \(2^n\) possible binary configurations for the Eggbox model.

  • Returns:
    • iterator: An iterator yielding all binary configurations (tuples of 0s and 1s) of length n.

docs.title: evaluate(config)
docs.text:
Calculates the fitness of a given binary configuration.

The fitness is determined by the sum of the elements in the configuration (\(s = \sum config_i\)), transformed by the function \(Fitness = \sin^2(\text{frequency} \cdot s \cdot \pi)\).

  • Parameters:
    • config (tuple | list): A binary configuration (e.g., (0, 1, 0, ..., 1)) of length n.
  • Returns:
    • float: The calculated fitness value (between 0 and 1, inclusive) for the configuration.
  • Raises:
    • ValueError: If the length of config does not match n.

def graphfla.analysis.classify_epistasis(landscape, approximate=False, sample_cut_prob=0.2) → Dict[str, float]

Calculates the prevalence of five types of epistasis in a fitness landscape. Epistatic interactions are classified into positive, negative, magnitude, sign, and reciprocal sign epistasis based on pairwise mutation analysis using the motif function in igraph.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Default Description landscape Landscape required The fitness landscape object. approximate bool False If True, estimates motif counts instead of exact enumeration. Recommended for large landscapes (>10,000 mutants). sample_cut_prob float 0.2 Pruning probability for the search tree. Only used when approximate=True. Higher = faster but less accurate. Must be in [0, 1].

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
Type Description Dict[str, float] Dictionary mapping 5 epistasis types to their proportions. Positive + negative sum to 1; magnitude + sign + reciprocal sign sum to 1.

Warning

Computationally expensive for large landscapes (>10,000 mutants). Use approximate=True with a higher sample_cut_prob value.

>>> classify_epistasis(landscape, approximate=True, sample_cut_prob=0.1)
{'magnitude epistasis': 0.909, 'sign epistasis': 0.076,
 'reciprocal sign epistasis': 0.016, 'positive epistasis': 0.365,
 'negative epistasis': 0.635}

def graphfla.analysis.classify_epistasis(landscape, approximate=False, sample_cut_prob=0.2) → Dict[str, float]

Calculates the prevalence of five types of epistasis in a fitness landscape.

DescriptionParametersReturnsExample

Epistatic interactions are classified into five types based on pairwise mutation analysis: positive, negative, magnitude, sign, and reciprocal sign epistasis. Uses the motif function in igraph internally.

Note

The fractions for positive and negative epistasis always sum to 1 (mutually exclusive and exhaustive). Likewise for magnitude, sign, and reciprocal sign.

Warning

Computationally expensive for large landscapes (>10,000 mutants). Use approximate=True for better performance.

Recommended for large landscapes (>10,000 mutants). sample_cut_prob : float, default=0.2 Pruning probability for the search tree. Only used when approximate=True. Higher values lead to faster computation but potentially less accurate estimations. Must be between 0 and 1. Key Range 'magnitude epistasis' [0, 1] 'sign epistasis' [0, 1] 'reciprocal sign epistasis' [0, 1] 'positive epistasis' [0, 1] 'negative epistasis' [0, 1]

def graphfla.analysis.classify_epistasis(landscape, approximate=False, sample_cut_prob=0.2) → Dict[str, float]

Calculates the prevalence of five types of epistasis in a fitness landscape. Epistatic interactions are classified into positive, negative, magnitude, sign, and reciprocal sign epistasis based on pairwise mutation analysis.

Parameters

landscape : Landscape — The fitness landscape object.

approximate : bool, default=False — If True, estimates motif counts instead of exact enumeration. Recommended for large landscapes (>10,000 mutants).

sample_cut_prob : float, default=0.2 — Pruning probability for the search tree (only when approximate=True). Higher = faster but less accurate. Must be in [0, 1].

Returns

Dict[str, float] — Dictionary mapping 5 epistasis types to their calculated proportions. Positive + negative sum to 1; magnitude + sign + reciprocal sign sum to 1.

Performance Note

This method can be quite computationally expensive for large landscapes (>10,000 mutants). Using approximate=True and a higher sample_cut_prob value is highly recommended.

Usage

from graphfla.analysis import classify_epistasis

result = classify_epistasis(landscape, approximate=True, sample_cut_prob=0.1)
# {'magnitude epistasis': 0.909, 'sign epistasis': 0.076,
#  'reciprocal sign epistasis': 0.016, 'positive epistasis': 0.365,
#  'negative epistasis': 0.635}

References

  • Patrick C. Phillips. "Epistasis—the essential role of gene interactions in the structure and evolution of genetic systems." Nat. Rev. Genet. (2008).
  • Frank J. Poelwijk et al., "Empirical fitness landscapes reveal accessible evolutionary paths." Nature. (2007).
