docs.title: Epistasis
docs.text:
asdas Epistasis---interactions among mutations that produce nonadditive effects on phenotype and fitness---has long been recognized as fundamentally important to understanding the structure and function of genetic pathways and the evolutionary dynamics of complex genetic systems. It plays a major role in evolution by determining the accessibility of mutational pathways and thereby influencing the rate of adaptation and the diversity and robustness of genetic mutants.

In simple terms, it describes the phenomenon that the fitness effect of a mutation depends on what other mutations are already present (i.e., genetic background).

docs.title: Basic Types of Epistasis
docs.text:
def graphfla.analysis.classify_epistasis(landscape, approximate=False, sample_cut_prob=0.2) → Dict[str, float]

Calculates the prevalence of five types of epistasis in a fitness landscape.

DescriptionParametersReturnsReferences

Specifically, epistatic interactions can be classified into (e.g., see Phillips 2008):

  • Negative epistasis: It occurs when the combined effect of two or more mutations on fitness is less favorable than would be predicted from their individual effects.
    • For deleterious mutations, it means their joint impact results in a synergistic fitness defect that is greater, or more severe, than expected. In extreme cases, this can lead to synthetic lethal or sick interactions, in which a combination of two individually viable mutations causes death (Wang et al. 2017).
    • For beneficial mutations, it means their combined beneficial effect on fitness is smaller than anticipated. This phenomenon is often described as diminishing-returns epistasis (see later) or antagonistic.
  • Positive epistasis: It occurs when the combined effect of two or more mutations on fitness is more favorable than would be predicted from their individual effects.
    • For beneficial mutations, this means their joint impact leads to a synergistic fitness increase that is greater than anticipated.
    • For deleterious mutations, this means their combined detrimental effect on fitness is less severe, or weaker, than expected (i.e., antagonistic). An extreme form of positive interaction is genetic suppression, where the double mutant exhibits better fitness than the least fit single mutant (Leeuwen et al. 2016).

Or (e.g., see Poelwijk 2007):

  • Magnitude epistasis: In magnitude epistasis, the effect of a mutation (e.g., beneficial or deleterious) maintains its sign (direction) regardless of the genetic background. However, the magnitude (strength) of this effect changes depending on what other mutations are present. For example, a mutation might be strongly beneficial in one genetic background but only weakly beneficial in another, yet it remains beneficial in both contexts. The fitness effects are non-additive, but the qualitative outcome (beneficial/deleterious) for a given mutation does not change.
  • Sign epistasis: Sign epistasis occurs when the effect of a mutation changes its sign (i.e., from beneficial to deleterious, or vice versa) depending on the genetic background (the presence of other mutations). A mutation that is advantageous in one genetic context might be detrimental in another, or a deleterious mutation might become beneficial when another specific mutation is present. This type of epistasis is significant because it can create rugged fitness landscapes with multiple fitness peaks, potentially trapping evolving populations at suboptimal states as some evolutionary paths become inaccessible.
  • Reciprocal sign epistasis: Reciprocal sign epistasis is a specific and stronger form of sign epistasis. It occurs when the sign of the fitness effect of each of two interacting mutations is reversed by the presence of the other mutation. A common scenario is where two mutations are individually deleterious (or less fit than the wild type), but their combination results in a genotype that is more fit than either single mutant, and potentially even more fit than the original wild type. This type of interaction is a necessary condition for the existence of multiple peaks on a fitness landscape.

This implementation calculates the fraction of each of these 5 types of epistasis among all pairs of mutations (i.e., pairwise epistasis) using the motif function in igraph.

Note

The fractions for positive and negative epistasis always sum to 1, as they are mutually exclusive and exhaustive. Likewise, the fractions for magnitude, sign, and reciprocal sign epistasis also sum to 1.

Warning

This method can be quite computationally expensive for large landscapes (e.g., >10,000 mutants). Using approximate=True and higher sample_cut_prob value is highly recommended.

landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object. approximate : bool, default=False
  • If True, the function will estimate motif counts instead of performing exact enumeration. This can significantly speed up computation for large graphs, but at the cost of precision.
  • If False (the default), exact motif counts are used. Not recommended for large landscapes (e.g., >10,000 mutants).
sample_cut_prob : float, default=0.2 This parameter is only relevant when approximate is True. It represents the probability used for pruning the search tree at each level during the sampling process for motif instances. Higher values will lead to faster computation but potentially less accurate estimations. The value must be between 0 and 1. dict[str, float] A dictionary where keys are 5 epistasis types and values are their calculated proportions.
  • Patrick C. Phillips. "Epistasis--the essential role of gene interactions in the structure and evolution of genetic systems." Nat. Rev. Genet. (2008).
  • Tim Wang, "Gene Essentiality Profiling Reveals Gene Networks and Synthetic Lethal Interactions with Oncogenic Ras." Cell. (2017).
  • Frank J. Poelwijk et al., "Empirical fitness landscapes reveal accessible evolutionary paths." Nature. (2007).
  • Jolanda van Leeuwen et al., "Exploring genetic suppression interactions on a global scale." Science (2016).

docs.title: Higher-order Epistasis
docs.text:
def graphfla.analysis.higher_order_epistasis(landscape, order: int = 2, random_state: int = 42, verbose: bool = False) → float

Measures the prevalence of higher-order epistasis in the landscape.

DescriptionParametersReturnsReferences

In addition to pairwise epistasis, higher-order epistasis that involves multiple mutations are also common in empirical fitness landscapes (e.g., Weinreich et al. 2013, Domingo et al. 2018). While traditional Fourier analysis can provide exact measures of all orders of epistasis, it is computationally prohibitive for relatively large landscapes (e.g., with 100,000 mutants).

As an alternative, GraphFLA provides a cheaper yet effective way for approximating the prevalence of high-order epistasis by seeing how much variance in fitness can be explained by considering epistasis up to a specific order.

This function fits a DecisionTreeRegressor from sklearn to the landscape data. The order of interaction used for fitting is controlled by max_depth of the tree and specified by the user with order. It returns the \(R^2\) (coefficient of determination) score of the model to indicate how much variance in fitness can be explained by interaction up to the specified order.

landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object. order : int, optional, default=2 The maximum order of variable interactions to consider. This parameter dictates the maximum depth of the decision tree used for modeling. For instance, an order of 2 allows for modeling pairwise interactions. It must be an integer between 1 and the total number of variables in the landscape. random_state : int, optional, default=42 A seed for the random number generator to ensure reproducibility of the decision tree fitting process. verbose : bool, optional, default=False If set to True, the function will print progress information during its execution, such as encoding steps and model fitting. float The \(R^2\) score, a value between 0.0 and 1.0, indicating the fraction of fitness variance explained by interactions up to the specified order. A higher value suggests stronger epistasis of the given order (and lower orders).
  • Daniel M Weinreich et al., "Should evolutionary geneticists worry about higher-order epistasis?" Curr. Opin. Genet. Dev. (2013).
  • Júlia Domingo et al., "Pairwise and higher-order genetic interactions during the evolution of a tRNA." Nature. (2018).

docs.title: Global Epistasis
docs.text:
Beyond interactions between specific pairs or sets of mutations, global epistasis describes a broader pattern where the fitness effect of a mutation systematically depends on the overall fitness of the genetic background in which it arises. Instead of the effect being determined by the presence or absence of one or a few specific other mutations, global epistasis implies a more general, often "nonspecific" or "unidimensional," relationship. While global epistasis can emerge from the sum of many specific, idiosyncratic interactions, it presents as an overarching trend.

Typically, global epistasis manifests as "fitness-correlated trends (FCTs)" as either diminishing returns for beneficial mutations or increasing costs for deleterious mutations, with mutations having a less positive or more negative effect on fitter backgrounds.

docs.title: Diminishing Returns Epistasis
docs.text:
def graphfla.analysis.diminishing_returns_index(landscape, method: Literal["pearson", "spearman", "regression"] = "pearson") → float

Measures diminishing returns epistasis in a fitness landscape.

DescriptionParametersReturnsReferencesExample

Diminishing returns epistasis is a specific form of global, and often negative, epistasis that applies to beneficial mutations. It describes the phenomenon where the positive fitness effect of a beneficial mutation is smaller when it occurs in a genetic background that is already highly fit (i.e., smoother up-hill paths), compared to its effect in a less fit background. As a population or genotype approaches a fitness peak, the incremental gains from new beneficial mutations tend to shrink, resulting in decelerated adaptation (Chou et al. 2011).

It calculates the correlation (or regression slope) between the fitness increase of beneficial mutations and the fitness of the genetic background in which they occur. A significant negative correlation (or a negative slope) indicates diminishing returns.

landscape : Landscape An initialized and built fitness landscape object. method : {'pearson', 'spearman', 'regression'}, default='pearson'

The method used to calculate the diminishing returns index:

  • 'pearson': Calculates the Pearson correlation coefficient.
  • 'spearman': Calculates the Spearman rank correlation coefficient.
  • 'regression': Calculates the slope of a linear regression between genotype fitnesses and average fitness improvements.
correlation or slope : float
  • For 'pearson' or 'spearman': The correlation coefficient between mutant fitness and average fitness improvement.
  • For 'regression': The slope of the linear regression.
  • Hsin-Hung Chou et al. "Diminishing returns epistasis among beneficial mutations decelerates adaptation." Science. (2011)
from graphfla.analysis import diminishing_returns_index

diminishing_returns_index(landscape, method="spearman")
# Output: -0.6735265536401572

docs.title: Increasing Cost Epistasis
docs.text:
def graphfla.analysis.increasing_costs_index(landscape, method: Literal["pearson", "spearman", "regression"] = "pearson") → float

Measures increasing cost epistasis in a fitness landscape.

DescriptionParametersReturns

Increasing cost epistasis is the analog of diminishing returns but for deleterious mutations. It describes a pattern where the negative fitness effect (or cost) of a deleterious mutation becomes more severe in genetic backgrounds that are already fitter (i.e., steeper down-hill paths). Conversely, in less fit genetic backgrounds, the same deleterious mutation might have a weaker detrimental effect.

It calculates the correlation (or regression slope) between the fitness decrease of deleterious mutations and the fitness of the genetic background in which they occur. A significant positive correlation (or a positive slope) indicates increasing cost.

landscape : Landscape An initialized and built fitness landscape object. method : {'pearson', 'spearman', 'regression'}, default='pearson'

The method used to calculate the increasing costs index:

  • 'pearson': Calculates the Pearson correlation coefficient.
  • 'spearman': Calculates the Spearman rank correlation coefficient.
  • 'regression': Calculates the slope of a linear regression between mutant fitnesses and average fitness costs.
correlation_or_slope : float
  • For 'pearson' or 'spearman': The correlation coefficient between mutant fitness and average fitness cost.
  • For 'regression': The slope of the linear regression.

docs.title: Gamma Statistic
docs.text:
def graphfla.analysis.gamma_statistic(landscape, n_jobs=-1) → Dict[str, float]

Calculates the gamma (\(\gamma\)) and gamma_star (\(\gamma^*\)) statistics for a fitness landscape (Ferretti et al., 2016).

DescriptionParametersReturnsReferences

The gamma statistic (\(\gamma\)) is a measure of the amount of epistasis in a fitness landscape by measuring the correlation between fitness effects of mutations across different genetic backgrounds. It is defined as the correlation of fitness effects of the same mutation in single-mutant neighbors. In simpler terms, it quantifies how the effect of a particular mutation is altered by the presence of another mutation at a different locus in the genetic background, averaged across the entire landscape.

  • \(\gamma = 1 \to\) no epistasis.
  • \(0 \leq \gamma < 1 \to\) magnitude epistasis, which would still result in a positive correlation between fitness effects, therefore \(\gamma\) would still be positive even if smaller than 1;

  • \(-\frac{1}{3} \leq \gamma < 0 \to\) sign epistasis, which would contribute with terms of both signs to the correlation, therefore resulting in values centered around 0:

  • \(-1 \leq \gamma -\frac{1}{3} \to\) reciprocal sign epistasis, which would imply a negative correlation between fitness effects, and therefore a negative value of \(\gamma\):

The \(\gamma^*\) is a variant of \(\gamma\) that focuses only on sign consistency, ignoring the magnitude of fitness effects. It indicates whether mutations tend to have consistent directional effects across different genetic backgrounds.

Note

The calculation involves iterating over pairs of positions and mutations to compare fitness effects across various genetic backgrounds. This process can be computationally intensive for landscapes with a large number of variables or many possible alleles at each position. Utilizing the n_jobs parameter for parallel processing is recommended for larger landscapes to improve performance.

landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object containing fitness data. It must be an initialized and built Landscape instance. n_jobs : int, optional, default=-1 Number of parallel jobs to use for the computation. A value of -1 utilizes all available CPU cores, which can significantly speed up calculations on multi-core machines. A value of 1 uses a single core (no parallelization). dict[str, float]

A dictionary containing the calculated statistics. Interpretations can be found above.

  • 'gamma' (float): The traditional gamma statistic value (\(\gamma\)).
  • 'gamma_star' (float): The gamma star statistic (\(\gamma^*\)) that only considers sign consistency.
  • Luca Ferretti et al., "Measuring epistasis in fitness landscapes: The correlation of fitness effects of mutations." J. Theor. Biol. (2016)

docs.title: Idiosyncratic Epistasis
docs.text:
def graphfla.analysis.global_idiosyncratic_index(landscape, random_state=None) → Dict[str, Union[float, Dict[str, float], int]]

In contrast to global epistasis, which describes a general, systematic trend where a mutation's effect correlates with overall background fitness, idiosyncratic epistasis refers to interactions where the fitness effect of a mutation is highly dependent on the specific other mutations present in the genetic background. Idiosyncratic epistasis emphasizes that these effects arise from particular, often unique, biological and physical interactions between specific loci. The "idiosyncrasy" means that the effect of a given mutation can vary substantially, and sometimes unpredictably, when combined with different sets of other mutations.

DescriptionParametersReturnsReferences

Calculates the global idiosyncratic index for the entire fitness landscape.

This function extends the concept of an idiosyncratic index for individual mutations, as proposed by Lyons et al. (2020), to a global measure for the entire landscape. It achieves this by averaging the idiosyncratic index across all possible single mutations within the landscape. The global idiosyncratic index quantifies the overall sensitivity of the landscape to idiosyncratic epistasis.

The index is defined as the variation in the fitness effect of a mutation across different genetic backgrounds, relative to the variation in fitness differences between random genotypes. It ranges from 0 to 1, where 0 indicates minimal idiosyncratic epistasis (the effect of a mutation is consistent across backgrounds) and 1 indicates maximum idiosyncratic epistasis (the effect of a mutation is highly variable and background-dependent).

landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object. random_state : int, optional, default=None A seed for the random number generator used within the calculation of individual idiosyncratic indices (specifically for sampling random fitness differences). Setting a seed ensures reproducibility of the results. dict

A dictionary containing the following keys:

  • 'global_index' (float): The overall idiosyncratic index for the landscape, calculated as the average of idiosyncratic indices over all valid mutations.
  • 'per_position' (dict[str, float]): A dictionary mapping each variable position (e.g., gene or site) to its average idiosyncratic index. The index for each position is the mean of idiosyncratic indices for all possible mutations at that position.
  • 'mutation_counts' (int): The total number of valid mutations for which the idiosyncratic index was successfully calculated and included in the global average.
  • Daniel M Lyons et al., "Idiosyncratic epistasis creates universals in mutational effects and evolutionary trajectories." Nat. Ecol. Evol. (2020).
  • Christopher W Bakerlee, "Idiosyncratic epistasis leads to global fitness-correlated trends." Science. (2022).

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
import pandas as pd
from graphfla.landscape import BooleanLandscape
from graphfla.problems import NK
from graphfla.analysis import (
    classify_epistasis,
    higher_order_epistasis,
    diminishing_returns_index, 
    increasing_costs_index,
    global_idiosyncratic_index
)

# demo landscape construction
problem = NK(4, 1)
data = problem.get_data()

X = data["config"].apply(pd.Series)
fitness = data["fitness"]

landscape = BooleanLandscape()
landscape = landscape.build_from_data(X, fitness, verbose=False)

# types of epistasis
>>> classify_epistasis(landscape, approximate=True, sample_cut_prob=0.1)
{'magnitude epistasis': 0.908663967611336,
 'sign epistasis': 0.07562753036437248,
 'reciprocal sign epistasis': 0.0157085020242915,
 'positive epistasis': 0.36534343600724756,
 'negative epistasis': 0.6346565639927524}

# higher-order epistasis
>>> higher_order_epistasis(landscape, order=3)
0.7116261680761315

# global epistasis
>>> diminishing_returns_index(landscape, method="spearman")
-0.6735265536401572
>>> increasing_costs_index(landscape, method="spearman")
0.5595161241004356

# idiosyncratic epistasis
>>> global_idiosyncratic_index(landscape)
{'global_index': 0.31631585751797814,
 'per_position': {'pos_0': 0.299281169353561,
                  'pos_1': 0.3603846643641,
                  'pos_2': 0.12046801346478977,
                  'pos_3': 0.48512958288946173},
 'mutation_counts': 4}

docs.title: Fitness
docs.text: Fitness is a fundamental component of fitness landscape. Without differences in fitness, natural selection cannot act and adaptation cannot occur. This module provides several tools to infer basic properties of the fitness distribution in the landscape.

docs.title: Fitness Distribution
docs.text:
def fitness_distribution(landscape: Landscape) -> Dict[str, Any]

Calculates statistics about the fitness distribution of the landscape.

While the fitness distribution is not, in the strict sense, a measure of the topography of fitness landscapes since it does not account for spatial geometry, it still provides valuable insights into the landscape. This function computes various statistics that characterize the shape and properties of the fitness distribution across the landscape. All statistics are unitless to allow meaningful comparisons across different landscapes with varying fitness scales.

Parameters

  • landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object.

Returns

  • Dict[str, Any] A dictionary containing the following statistics:
    • skewness: Measure of asymmetry of the fitness distribution.
    • kurtosis: Measure of "tailedness" of the fitness distribution.
    • cv: Coefficient of variation (ratio of standard deviation to mean).
    • quartile_coefficient: Interquartile range divided by median (IQR/median).
    • median_mean_ratio: Ratio of median to mean.
    • relative_range: Range divided by median.
    • cauchy_loc: Location parameter (center) of the fitted Cauchy distribution.

Note

  • Skewness > 0 indicates a right-skewed distribution (tail on the right).
  • Skewness < 0 indicates a left-skewed distribution (tail on the left).
  • Kurtosis > 3 (Pearson's definition) indicates heavy tails and a peaked distribution relative to a normal distribution. The function converts SciPy's Fisher's definition (normal kurtosis = 0) to Pearson's.
  • Kurtosis < 3 indicates light tails and a flatter distribution.
  • A higher Coefficient of Variation (CV) indicates greater relative dispersion.
  • The Cauchy location parameter represents the center or peak of the fitted Cauchy distribution. This can be useful for characterizing heavy-tailed distributions where moments like mean and variance may not be well-defined.

docs.title: Distribution of Fitness Effects
docs.text:
def distribution_fit_effects(landscape: Landscape, mutation: tuple) -> list

Calculates the distribution of fitness effects (DFE) for a specific mutation across all possible genetic backgrounds.

Every mutation would have a fitness effect-be it beneficial, detrimental, or neutral. Yet this fitness effect is not an intrinsic property of a mutation alone but is highly dependent on the genetic background in which it occurs: Due to the existence of sign epistasis, the same mutation can frequently switch from detrimental to beneficial, even in closely related genotypes, and the DFE of combined mutations is not simply the sum or product of individual effects.

This function measures how the effect of a specific mutation varies depending on the genetic context (background) in which it occurs. It returns a list of fitness differences caused by the mutation across different genetic context.

Parameters

  • landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object.
  • mutation : tuple(A, pos, B) A tuple containing:
    • A: The original variable value (allele) at the given position.
    • pos: The position (index) in the genotype where the mutation occurs.
    • B: The new variable value (allele) after the mutation.

Returns

  • list A list of fitness effect values for the mutation across different genetic backgrounds. Each value represents the fitness difference between a genotype and its mutant carrying the specified mutation in a specific genetic background. An empty list is returned if no common backgrounds are found.

docs.title: Navigability
docs.text:
Landscape navigability, often closely related to peak accessibility, concerns the ease with which evolving populations can traverse a fitness landscape to discover and ascend fitness peaks, particularly those representing high or optimal fitness genotypes. It is a critical property influencing the rate and predictability of adaptation, as it determines whether populations can efficiently find beneficial evolutionary trajectories or become trapped on suboptimal solutions. The fundamental basis of navigability lies in the existence and nature of accessible paths—sequences of mutations where each step confers a non-decreasing, and typically increasing, fitness effect, which natural selection can favor.

In simple terms, it describes whether there are viable mutational routes that an evolving population can follow to reach higher fitness states without having to cross significant fitness valleys, which selection would typically prevent.

docs.title: Peak Accessibility
docs.text:
def graphfla.analysis.local_optima_accessibility(landscape, lo: Union[int, List[int]]) -> Union[float, List[float]]

Calculates the accessibility of one or more specified peak(s) in the fitness landscape.

This metric quantifies the proportion of all genotypes in the landscape that can reach a specified peak (or set of peaks) by following any path of monotonically increasing fitness (i.e., adaptive walks). It is equivalent to the size of the peak’s basin of attraction divided by the total number of genotypes in the landscape. In other words, it reflects how many variants fall within the basin of attraction of the given peak(s).

A higher accessibility indicates that more variants can access the peak(s) during evolution, whereas a low value indicates that the peak(s) is hardly accessible.

Parameters

  • landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object.

  • lo : Union[int, List[int]] The index of the peak to analyze, or a list of indices if analyzing multiple peaks. Each index must correspond to a genotype that is a true peak (i.e., is_lo=True).

Returns

  • Union[float, List[float]]
    • If lo is a single integer: Returns a single float value (between 0.0 and 1.0) representing the accessibility.
    • If lo is a list of integers: Returns a list of floats, where each float corresponds to the accessibility of the peak at the respective index in the input list lo.

def graphfla.analysis.global_optima_accessibility(landscape) -> float

Calculates the accessibility of the global peak in the fitness landscape.

This metric quantifies the proportion of all genotypes in the landscape that can reach the global peak via any path of monotonically increasing fitness (i.e., adaptive walks). It is equivalent to the size of the gloal peak’s basin of attraction divided by the total number of genotypes in the landscape. In other words, it reflects how many variants fall within the basin of attraction of the global peak.

Since the global peak is the utmost goal of evolution, its accessibility can reflect the navigability of whole landscape.

Parameters

  • landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object.

Returns

  • float The fraction of genotypes (a value between 0.0 and 1.0) from which the global peak can be reached through monotonic, fitness-improving paths.

docs.title: Fitness Distance Correlation
docs.text:
def graphfla.analysis.fitness_distance_corr(landscape, method: str = "spearman") -> float

Calculates the Fitness Distance Correlation (FDC) of a landscape.

This metric measures the navigability of a fitness landscape by quantifying the correlation between the fitness values of variants and their respective distances to the global peak. It assesses how informative the fitness landscape is in guiding the evolution towards prominent regions. A landscape where fitness reliably increases as evolution approaches the global peak (indicated by a strong negative FDC) is generally considered easier to navigate than one where the relationship is weak, random, or misleading (indicated by an FDC near zero or positive).

Automatic Distance Calculation

The function automatically attempts to calculate the distance to the global peak for each genotype if it's not already present in the landscape data . This will add new attributes for genotypes ("dist_go") that can be accessed via Landscape.graph or Landscape.get_data().

Parameters

  • landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object.

  • method : {'spearman', 'pearson'}, default='spearman' The statistical correlation measure to be used for assessing FDC:

    • 'spearman': Calculates Spearman's rank correlation coefficient.
    • 'pearson': Calculates Pearson correlation coefficient.

Returns

  • float The calculated Fitness Distance Correlation coefficient. This value ranges from -1 to 1.
    • A value close to -1 indicates that fitness tends to increase as the distance to the global peak decreases, corresponding to a highly navigable landscape.
    • A value close to 0 implies a more rugged, neutral, or unpredictable relationship between fitness and distance to the global peak, resulting in reduced navigability.
    • A value close to +1 indicates fitness tends to decrease as the distance to the global peak decreases. This suggests a "deceptive" landscape where regions of higher fitness are generally further away from the actual global peak, making it extremely difficult for evolution to navigate.

References

  • Terry Jones and Stephanie Forrest, "Fitness Distance Correlation as a Measure of Problem Difficulty for Genetic Algorithms." In Proceedings of the Sixth International Conference on Genetic Algorithms (ICGA'95) (1995).

docs.title: Basin Size-Fitness Correlation
docs.text:
def graphfla.analysis.basin_fit_corr(landscape, method: str = "spearman") -> Dict[str, float]

Calculates the correlation between the size of the basin of attraction and the fitness of peaks.

The size of the basin of attraction of a peak is defined as the total number of variants in the landscape from which the peak is accessible. The size of these basins can reveal important information about the landscape's structure and navigability. A common question is whether larger basins tend to be associated with fitter peaks, which could imply that fitter peaks are easier to be accessed. This function quantifies such a relationship by calculating the correlation between basin sizes and the fitness values of their corresponding peaks.

Parameters

  • landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object.

  • method : str, one of {"spearman", "pearson"}, default='spearman' The correlation measure to use:

    • 'spearman': Computes Spearman's rank correlation coefficient. This method assesses monotonic relationships and is robust to outliers.
    • 'pearson': Computes Pearson's product-moment correlation coefficient. This method assesses linear relationships and assumes data is approximately normally distributed.

Returns

  • dict[str, float] A dictionary containing the correlation coefficient(s). The correlation coefficient is a value between -1 and 1. A larger value (e.g., close to 1) implies suggests that fitter peaks tend to have larger basin of attraction, indicating a more navigable landscape.
    • If only greedy basin sizes are available or calculated, the dictionary will be: {'greedy': correlation_coefficient}.
    • If both greedy and accessible basin sizes are available, the dictionary will be: {'greedy': correlation_coefficient_greedy, 'accessible': correlation_coefficient_accessible}.

Automatic Basin Calculation

If basin sizes are not already computed and stored in the landscape object's graph vertex attributes, this function will attempt to calculate them by calling landscape.determine_basin_of_attraction().

docs.title: Evolvability-enhancing Mutations
docs.text:
def graphfla.analysis.calculate_evol_enhance(landscape: Landscape, epsilon: float = 0, auto_calculate: bool = True) -> Dict[str, Union[float, int]]

Calculates the prevalence of evolvability-enhancing (EE) mutations within the fitness landscape.

Wagner defines the concept of an evolvability-enhancing (EE) mutation to be a mutation that creates a genetic background in which subsequent mutations are more likely to be adaptive. In other words, an EE mutation increases the average fitness of neighboring genotypes compared to the original background. This function calculates the proportion of all fitness-increasing mutations in the landscapes that are also EE.

Automatic Calculation of Neighbor Fitness

If the average neighbor fitness of each variant is not calculated for landscape, this function will automatically call landscape.calculate_neighbor_fitness() to compute it. This will create new attributes for each variant that can be accessed via Landscape.graph or Landscape.get_data(). It will also add a new edge attribute to Landscape.graph that indicates the difference in average neighbor fitness between single mutants.

Parameters

  • landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object.

  • epsilon : float, default=0 A tolerance threshold for detecting significant evolvability enhancement. A mutation is counted as EE if its resulting improvement in average neighbor fitness is larger than this value.

Returns

  • Dict[str, Union[float, int]] A dictionary containing the following keys:
    • 'ee_proportion' (float): The proportion of edges in the landscape that are classified as EE.
    • 'ee_count' (int): The absolute count of EE mutations.
    • 'total_edges' (int): The total number of single-point mutations in the landscape.

References

  • Andreas Wagner, "Evolvability-enhancing mutations in the fitness landscapes of an RNA and a protein." Nat Commun. (2023).

docs.title: Length of Accessible Paths
docs.text:
def graphfla.analysis.mean_path_lengths(landscape, lo: Union[int, List[int]] = None, accessible: bool = True, n_samples: Optional[Union[int, float]] = None) -> Union[dict, List[dict]]

Calculates the mean and variance of the shortest path lengths from variants to specified peaks.

In a perfectly smooth landscape, the length of the shortest accessible path from any one variant to a high fitness peak equals the genetic distance between the variant and the peak. In contrast, in a rugged landscape, even the shortest accessible path may meander through the landscape and thus be much longer than this genetic distance.

This function quantifies these path lengths, providing insights into the landscape's navigability by measuring the expected adaptive walk steps required to reach peaks. It computes the shortest path length from each variant (or a sample thereof) to one or more target peaks.

Parameters

  • landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object.

  • lo : Union[int, List[int]], optional The index (or list of indices) of the peak(s) to analyze. Each index must correspond to a node that is a valid peak. If None (default), the function will use the global peak of the landscape.

  • n_samples : Optional[Union[int, float]], default=None Specifies whether to use sampling to approximate the results, which is recommended for large landscapes:

    • If a float between 0.0 (exclusive) and 1.0 (inclusive): Samples this fraction of the total variants.
    • If an int greater than 1: Samples this specific number of variants.
    • If None: Computes path lengths for all variants. A warning is issued if the number of variants exceeds 10,000.

Returns

  • Union[dict, List[dict]]
    • If lo is a single integer (or None, targeting the global peak): A dictionary containing the "mean" and "variance" of the shortest path lengths to that optimum.
    • If lo is a list of integers: A list of dictionaries, where each dictionary corresponds to a peak in the input list lo and contains its respective "mean" and "variance". Path lengths that are infinite (i.e., the optimum is unreachable from a variant under the given accessible constraint) are excluded from the mean and variance calculations. If no finite paths exist to an optimum, np.nan will be returned for mean and variance.

Computational Cost on Large Landscapes

For landscapes with a large number of variants (e.g., >10,000), calculating all-pairs shortest paths can be very time-consuming and memory-intensive. It is highly recommended to use the n_samples parameter to analyze a subset of variants in such cases.

def graphfla.analysis.mean_path_lengths_go(landscape, accessible: bool = True, n_samples: Optional[Union[int, float]] = None) -> dict

Calculates the mean and variance of the shortest path lengths from variants to the global peak.

This function computes the shortest path length from each variant (or a sample thereof) to the global peak of the landscape. It serves as a convenience wrapper around the more general mean_path_lengths function, specifically targeting the global peak. The path lengths provide insights into the landscape's navigability by measuring the expected number of adaptive steps required to reach the global peak.

Parameters

  • landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object.
  • n_samples : Optional[Union[int, float]], default=None Specifies whether to use sampling to approximate the results, recommended for large landscapes:
    • If a float between 0.0 (exclusive) and 1.0 (inclusive): Samples this fraction of the total variants.
    • If an int greater than 1: Samples this specific number of variants.
    • If None: Computes path lengths for all variants. A warning similar to that in mean_path_lengths applies if the number of variants is large.

Returns

  • dict A dictionary containing the "mean" and "variance" of the shortest path lengths to the global peak. Path lengths that are infinite (i.e., the global peak is unreachable from a variant) are excluded from the mean and variance calculations. If no finite paths exist to the global peak, np.nan will be returned for mean and variance.

Computational Cost on Large Landscapes

For landscapes with a large number of variants (e.g., >10,000), calculating shortest paths can be very time-consuming and memory-intensive. It is highly recommended to use the n_samples parameter to analyze a subset of variants in such cases.

def graphfla.analysis.mean_dist_lo(landscape, lo: Union[int, List[int]], distance_func: Optional[Callable] = None) -> Union[float, List[float]]

Calculates the mean distance from all variants to one or more specified peak(s).

This function provides a measure of how "far" on average other points in the landscape are from specific peak(s), using a defined distance metric (e.g., Hamming distance or Edit distance). This can be useful for understanding the global structure of the landscape in relation to its peaks.

Parameters

  • landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object.
  • lo : Union[int, List[int]] The index (or a list of indices) of the peak(s) to analyze. Each provided index must correspond to a node in the landscape graph that is a valid peak.
  • distance_func : Optional[Callable], default=None A callable function used to calculate the distance between variants. The function should typically accept two variants (or arrays of variants) and landscape data types as input and return a numerical distance or an array of distances. If None, the landscape's default distance metric (e.g., Hamming distance) will be used.

Returns

  • Union[float, List[float]]
    • If lo is a single integer: Returns a float representing the mean distance from all variants in the landscape to the specified peak.
    • If lo is a list of integers: Returns a list of floats, where each float is the mean distance to the corresponding peak in the input list lo.

def graphfla.analysis.mean_dist_go(landscape, distance_func: Optional[Callable] = None) -> float

Calculates the mean distance from all variants to the global peak.

This function determines the average distance from every variant in the landscape to its global peak. It can utilize a pre-calculated 'dist_go' vertex attribute if available; otherwise, it computes these distances using the specified or default distance function. This metric helps characterize the overall spread or compactness of the landscape relative to its highest peak.

Parameters

  • landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object.
  • distance_func : Optional[Callable], default=None A callable function to calculate distances between variants. If None, the landscape's default distance metric is used.

Returns

  • float The mean distance from all variants in the landscape to the global peak.

Automatic Distance Calculation

The function automatically attempts to calculate the distance to the global peak for each genotype if it's not already present in the landscape data . This will add new attributes for genotypes ("dist_go") that can be accessed via Landscape.graph or Landscape.get_data().

docs.title: Neutrality
docs.text:
def neutrality(landscape, threshold: float = 0.01) -> float

Calculates the overall neutrality of the landscape.

Landscape neutrality describes the presence of regions where distinct genotypes exhibit equivalent or very similar fitness levels. Mutations that result in negligible or no change in fitness are known as neutral mutations, and interconnected sets of such genotypes form neutral networks or effectively neutral regions. These neutral pathways enable populations to explore a wide array of genetic variations without incurring significant fitness penalties.

This function measures the overall neutrality of the landscape by calculating the proportion of single-point mutations in the landscape whose fitness effects fall below a specified threshold. A higher neutrality value suggests larger "neutral networks" or areas where mutations have minimal impact on fitness.

Parameters

  • landscape : object The fitness landscape object.
  • threshold : float, default=0.01 The noise tolerance threshold. Mutations with fitness effects smaller than this value will be considered neutral.

Returns

  • neutrality : float The neutrality index, a value between 0.0 and 1.0.
    • A value closer to 1.0 indicates a highly neutral landscape, wherein most mutations have negligible fitness effects (thus most genotypes would have similar fitness)
    • A value closer to 0.0 indicates a landscape where neutral mutations are rare.

docs.title: Mutational Robustness
docs.text: On the individual genotype level, mutational robustness refers to the ability of a genotype to preserve its phenotype (fitness) when subjected to mutations (perturbations). Genotypes exhibiting high mutational robustness can endure a greater proportion of mutational changes with minimal or no adverse effects. Such genotypes often occupy "flatter" areas of the fitness landscape, and under conditions of high mutation rates, selection might favor these robust genotypes through a mechanism termed "survival of the flattest."

docs.title: Ruggedness
docs.text:
Ruggedness is arguably the most widely studied topograhical aspect of fitness landscapes. Although biologists have offered a staggering number of definitions of ruggedness, they agree broadly on the essence of the idea: the lack of correlation in fitness between genotypes. This can either manifests as the presence of multiple fitness peaks (local optima) or significant fitness fluctuation.

Relationship to landscape navigability: Ruggedness can pose a fundamental challenge to evolution’s ability to find a landscape’s highest peaks, i.e., landscape navigability or peak accessibility. This is because a population evolving under the influence of natural selection can only travel on accessible paths through the landscape, that is, paths in which each mutational step increases fitness. The reason is that natural selection favors high fitness genotypes and does not allow a population to traverse low fitness valleys between a local peak of intermediate fitness and nearby higher fitness peaks.

  • In a smooth landscpae, all mutational paths to the single peak are accessible.
  • When landscape becomes rugged, many peaks exist, yet high-fitness peaks are reachable by abundant short accessible paths, especially in high-dimensional sequence spaces.
  • For a maximumally rugged landscape, selectively accessible paths become rare, and evolution may stall on suboptimal peaks.

Relationship to epistasis: Reciprocal sign epistasis is a necessary yet not sufficient condition for landscape ruggedness-landscape can be rugged even when it is rare, while its mere presence does not guarantee ruggedness. Yet, for a nonepistatic (i.e., purely additive) landscape, there is often only a single, global fitness peak. In contrast, pervasive sign epistasis could create a rugged landscape with numerous sub-optimal peaks.

Following the key idea of ruggedness, various established quantitative measures have been developed.

docs.title: Local Optima
docs.text:
def graphfla.analysis.lo_ratio(landscape) -> float

Calculates the proportion of local optima variants in the landscape.

The number of local optima (peaks) is a principal indicator of fitness landscape ruggedness. To make it a unitless measure, we divide it by the number of total variants in the landscape. To provide a sense of its magnitude, a mostly rugged NK landscape with dimension \(n\) and degree of interaction \(k=n-1\) would have \(\frac{2^n}{n+1}\) local optima. When \(n=20\), the ratio of local optima would be around \(4.76\%\).

Parameters

  • landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object.

Returns

  • float The ratio of local optima, ranging from 0 to 1. Larger values indicate more rugged landscapes.

docs.title: Roughness-to-Slope (r/s) Ratio
docs.text:
def graphfla.analysis.r_s_ratio(landscape) -> float

Calculates the roughness-to-slope (r/s) ratio of a fitness landscape.

The Roughness-to-Slope (r/s) ratio is an overall measure of the ruggedness of a fitness landscape that quantifies how well the landscape can be described by a purely additive model, where the effects of individual mutations sum linearly. It is calculated by fitting the fitness landscape to a multidimensional linear model using the least-squares method. The slope of the linear model corresponds to the average additive fitness effect, whereas the roughness is given by the variance of the residuals.

Generally, the better the linear model fit, the smaller the variance in residuals such that the roughness-to-slope ratio approaches 0 in a perfectly additive model (i.e., smooth landscape). Conversely, a very rugged fitness landscape would have a large residual variance and, thus, a very large roughness-to-slope ratio.

Parameters

  • landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object.

Returns

  • float The calculated roughness-to-slope (\(r/s\)) ratio.

Underdetermined or Unstable Regression

If the number of samples (genotypes) in the landscape data is less than or equal to the number of features (after encoding categorical/boolean variables) used for the linear regression, a warning is issued. In such cases, the linear regression model might be underdetermined or unstable, potentially affecting the reliability of the \(r/s\) ratio.

Zero or Near-Zero Slope

If the calculated slope (\(s\)) is zero or extremely close to zero, it suggests that the landscape is either flat or that the additive model explains very little of the fitness variation (implying strong epistasis or noise dominating any additive signal). In this scenario, the function returns numpy.inf for the \(r/s\) ratio and issues a warning.

docs.title: Autocorrelation
docs.text:
def graphfla.analysis.autocorrelation(landscape, walk_length: int = 10, walk_times: int = 1000, lag: int = 1) -> float

Calculates the autocorrelation of a fitness landscape (Weinberger 1990).

Autocorrelation measures landscape ruggedness by simulating random walks across the landscape. For a smooth landscape, fitness values for adjacent variants encountered during the same walk would be highly correlated (autocorrelation close to 1). In contrasts, this correlation diminishes in rugged landscapes wherein fitness values fluctuates dramatically even across a single mutation (autocorrelation close to 0).

Parameters

  • landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object.

  • walk_length : int, default=10 The number of steps taken in each individual random walk. Recommend to be slightly shorted than the dimension of the landscape (i.e., length of each sequence).

  • walk_times : int, default=1000 The total number of independent random walks to perform on the landscape. It controls the noise in the measure, where more repeatations leads to in more reliable measurement.

  • lag : int, default=1 The distance (number of steps) lag used for calculating the autocorrelation within each walk. For example, a lag of 1 compares fitness at step \(i\) with fitness at step \(i+1\).

Returns

  • autocorr : float The mean of the autocorrelation values calculated across all random walks. Values close to 0 indicates a fairly rugged landscape.

References

  • E. Weinberger, "Correlated and Uncorrelated Fitness Landscapes and How to Tell the Difference," Biol. Cybern. 63, 325-336 (1990).

docs.title: Neighbor Fitness Correlation
docs.text:
def graphfla.analysis.neighbor_fit_corr(landscape: BaseLandscape, auto_calculate: bool = True, method: Literal["pearson", "spearman", "kendall"] = "pearson") -> Dict

Calculates the correlation between a configuration's fitness and the mean fitness of its neighbors across the fitness landscape.

Neighbor fitness correlation (NFC) reflects how fitness values are distributed spatially. A strong positive correlation indicates that high-fitness configurations tend to be surrounded by other high-fitness neighbors—and similarly for low-fitness ones—suggesting clustering, separability, and an underlying gradient in the landscape. In contrast, a weak or negative correlation implies that high- and low-fitness configurations are intermixed, signaling high fitness variability and a rugged, unstructured landscape.

Parameters

  • landscape : Landscape The fitness landscape object.

  • auto_calculate : bool, default=True If True, the function will automatically call Landscape.determine_neighbor_fitness() if the mean neighbor fitness values haven't been pre-calculated. If False, it will raise a RuntimeError if these metrics are missing.

  • method : Literal["pearson", "spearman", "kendall"], default='pearson' The statistical method to use for calculating the correlation:

    • 'pearson': Computes Pearson's product-moment correlation coefficient. Assumes data is normally distributed.
    • 'spearman': Computes Spearman's rank correlation coefficient. Based on ranked values, suitable for non-normally distributed data and monotonic relationships.
    • 'kendall': Computes Kendall's Tau rank correlation coefficient. Also based on ranks, often used for smaller datasets or when there are many tied ranks.

Returns

  • NFC: float The neighbor fitness correlation (NFC) of the landscape. Lower and negative value indicates a less structured, rugged landscape with large fitness fluctuations.

docs.title: DNA Landscape
docs.text: hello

docs.title: graphfla.Landscape
docs.text:
class graphfla.landscape.Landscape(type='default', maximize=True)

Class implementing the fitness landscape object.

This class provides a foundational structure for fitness landscapes, conceptualized as a mapping from genotype (configuration) space to fitness values. It represents the landscape as a directed graph (Smith 1970, Aguilar-Rodríguez 2017) where nodes are genotypes and edges connect mutational neighbors, which always point towards fitter variants. Fitness values (and other potential attributes) are stored as node attributes. Fitness differences between neighboring genotypes are stored as edge weights.

The landscape can be either constructed from raw data (using build_from_data()) or leveraging an existing graph (using build_from_graph()).

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• type : {'default', 'boolean', 'dna', 'rna', 'protein', 'sequence'}, default='default' The type of landscape to create.

    • With 'default' it implements a general landscape object that can actually handle all types of data (i.e., ordinal, categorical or boolean). But data_types in build_from_data must be explicitly specified since graphfla does not know what types of data it is handling.
    • With specialized types, i.e., 'boolean', 'dna', 'rna', or 'protein', no explicit data type declariation is needed as they are predefined in graphfla. This would also allow improved performance and more flexible input data formats (see build_from_data). Using these types with Landscape class is equivalent to initializing a specialized landscape class, e.g., DNALandscape.
    • With "sequence", this essentially does the same as SequenceLandscape, which can take performance advantages like with the pre-defined special types above while allow customized search space to handle other problems (e.g., customized chemical space).

  • maximize : bool, default=True Determines the optimization direction. If True, the landscape seeks higher fitness values (peaks are optima). If False, it seeks lower fitness values (valleys are optima). This affects edge direction in the graph and identification of optima.

docs.title: Attributes
docs.text:
• graph : igraph.Graph The directed igraph.Graph representing the fitness landscape. Nodes represent configurations (genotypes) and edges connect neighboring configurations, typically pointing from lower to higher fitness if maximize is True. Each node usually has a 'fitness' attribute. Populated after calling build_from_data or build_from_graph. Fitness difference between neighboring nodes is stored in the 'delta_fit' edge attribute. Running some analyses, e.g., determine_basin_of_attraction() can result in further node attributes, e.g., "basin_size_greedy".

  • configs : pandas.Series A pandas Series mapping node indices (int) to their corresponding configuration representation (often a tuple). This represents the genotypes in the landscape. Populated after calling build_from_data.

  • config_dict : dict A dictionary describing the encoding scheme for configuration variables. Keys are typically integer indices of variables, and values are dictionaries specifying properties like 'type' (e.g., 'boolean', 'categorical') and 'max' (maximum encoded value). Populated after calling build_from_data.

  • data_types : dict A dictionary specifying the data type for each variable in the configuration space (e.g., {'var_0': 'boolean', 'var_1': 'categorical'}). Validated and stored during build_from_data. Required for certain distance calculations and type-specific processing.

  • n_configs : int The total number of configurations (nodes) in the landscape graph. Populated after calling build_from_data or build_from_graph.

  • n_vars : int The number of variables (dimensions) defining a configuration in the genotype space. Populated after calling build_from_data or inferred by build_from_graph.

  • shape : tuple The shape (n_configs, n_edges) of the landscape graph as a tuple (number_of_nodes, number_of_edges).

  • n_edges : int The total number of directed edges (connections) in the landscape graph. Populated after calling build_from_data or build_from_graph.

  • n_lo : int The number of local optima (peaks or valleys depending on maximize) in the landscape. Local optima are nodes without fitter neighbors. Populated after graph analysis within build_from_data or build_from_graph.

  • lo : list[int] A sorted list of the node indices corresponding to local optima. Populated after graph analysis.

  • go : int The node index of the global optimum (the configuration with the highest or lowest fitness). Populated after graph analysis.

  • go : dict A dictionary containing the attributes (including 'fitness') of the global optimum node. Populated after graph analysis.

  • lon : igraph.Graph The Local Optima Network (LON) (Verel 2010) graph, if calculated via get_lon. Nodes in the LON are local optima from the main landscape graph, and edges represent accessibility between their basins

  • has_lon : bool Flag indicating whether the LON has been calculated and stored in the lon attribute. Updated by get_lon.

  • maximize : bool, default=True Indicates whether the objective is to maximize (True) or minimize (False) the fitness values. Set during initialization or inferred from graph.

  • epsilon : float or None, default=None Tolerance for fitness comparison. Fitness difference less than epsilon will be considered neutral. Set during build_from_data.

  • verbose : bool The verbosity level set during initialization or construction. Controls printed output during operations like building or analysis.

  • shape : tuple Returns the shape (n_configs, n_edges) of the landscape graph. Accessible via the shape property.

  • _is_built : bool Internal flag indicating if the landscape has been populated via build_from_data or build_from_graph.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• J. Maynard Smith, "Natural selection and the concept of a protein space", Nature (1970).
  • José Aguilar-Rodríguez et al., "A thousand empirical adaptive landscapes and their navigability", Nat. Ecol. Evol., (2017).
  • Sébastien Verel et al., "Local optima networks of NK landscapes with neutrality", IEEE Trans. Evol. Comput. (2010).
  • Andrei Papkou et al., "A rugged yet easily navigable fitness landscape", Science (2023).
  • Andreas Wagner, "Evolvability-enhancing mutations in the fitness landscapes of an RNA and a protein", Nat. Commun (2023).
  • Darrell Whitley et al., "Greedy or Not? Best Improving versus First Improving Stochastic Local Search for MAXSAT", AAAI (2013).

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
>>> from graphfla.landscape import Landscape
>>> import pandas as pd

# Example Data (Boolean landscape)
>>> X = pd.DataFrame({
        'var_0': [0, 0, 1, 1], 
        'var_1': [0, 1, 0, 1]
    })
>>> f = pd.Series([
        1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 2.5
    ])
>>> data_types_dict = {'var_0': 'boolean', 'var_1': 'boolean'}

>>> # Initialize and build the landscape
>>> landscape = Landscape(type="boolean", maximize=True)
>>> landscape.build_from_data(
        X, f, 
        data_types=data_types_dict, 
        calculate_basins=True,
        calculate_distance=True
        calculate_accessibility=False,
        calculate_neighbor_fit=False,
        verbose=False
    )

>>> print(f"Number of configurations: {landscape.n_configs}")
Number of configurations: 4
>>> print(f"Global optimum fitness: {landscape.go['fitness']}")
Global optimum fitness: 3.0

>>> # Get data as DataFrame
>>> df = landscape.get_data()
>>> print(df.head())
   var_0  var_1  fitness  is_lo  basin_index  dist_go
0      0      0      1.0  False            2        2
1      0      1      2.0  False            2        1
2      1      0      3.0   True            2        1
3      1      1      2.5  False            2        1

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
.build_from_data(X, f, attributes=None, data_types=None, epsilon=None, calculate_basins=True, calculate_accessibility=True, calculate_distance=True, calculate_neighbor_fit=True, impute_model=None, filter=None, giant_component=True, verbose=None) -> None

Construct the landscape graph and properties from configuration data. This method populates the core attributes of the Landscape instance and enable further analysis.

This method takes genotype-phenotype data (configurations X and their corresponding fitness values f) and builds the underlying graph structure of the fitness landscape. Nodes represent configurations, and edges connect neighbors that differ at only one bit. It then calculates various basic graph/landscape properties like node degrees, local optima, the global optimum, and various advanced features if specified (e.g., with calculate_distance=True the distance of each configuration to the global optimum will be calculated).

Warning

Either build_from_data or build_from_graph must be called before any analysis or visualization methods can be applied to a Landscape object.

Note

After landscape construction, indices of X and f will be reset to range(len(X)), and f will be named as "fitness" regardless of what they are in the inputs.

Parameters

  • X : pandas.DataFrame, list[list] or numpy.ndarray The configuration data, where each row represents a genotype or configuration, and columns represent variables or sites.

  • f : pandas.Series, list, or numpy.ndarray The fitness values corresponding to each configuration in X. Must have the same length as X.

  • attributes : pandas.Series, list, pandas.DataFrame, list[list], or numpy.ndarray Additional attribute(s) (e.g., a boolean flag "is_functional") that provide(s) additional information for each configuration.

  • data_types : dict[str, str], optional A dictionary specifying the type of each variable (column in X if DataFrame, or inferred column index if ndarray). Keys must match column names/indices, and values must be one of 'boolean', 'categorical', or 'ordinal'. Required if type='default' is used when initializing the landscape, otherwise inferred or generated automatically.

  • epsilon : float, optional Noise tolerance \(\epsilon\) used for fitness comparisons. Affects whether small fitness differences are considered neutral or directional, for example, \(\Delta f = f(\mathbf{x}) - f(\mathbf{x}^\prime)\) will be considered 0 (i.e., "neutral") if \(\Delta f < \epsilon\). Typically, a larger \(\epsilon\) would smoothen a landscape that could have been rugged (potentially due to noise in fitness measure) (Aguilar-Rodríguez 2017). In literature, the setting of \(\epsilon\) can be grounded on the variance in fitness measures across replications (Aguilar-Rodríguez 2017).

  • calculate_basins : bool, default=True If True, calculates the basins of attraction for each local optimum by calling determine_basin_of_attration(). Populates the node attribute basin_index specifying the basin of the local optimum in which each configuration is located; and size_basin_greedy and radius_basin_greedy for local optima configurations .

  • calculate_accessibility : bool, default=True If True, calculates the number of configurations from which the global optimum can be reached via monotonic, fitness-increasing paths by calling determine_accessible_paths(). Populates the size_basin_accessible node attribute. This basin size is the type analyzed in (Papkou 2023). Note that this method can be computationally intensive for large landscapes (e.g., >300,000 configurations) with many local optima.

  • calculate_distance : bool, default=True If True, calculates the distance (e.g., hamming distance by default for type='DNA') between each node and the global optimum by calling determine_dist_to_go(). Populates the dist_go node attribute.

  • calculate_neighbor_fit : bool, default=True If True, calculates the mean fitness of neighbors for each node (mean_neighbor_fit attribute) and the difference in this mean between connected nodes (delta_mean_neighbor_fit edge attribute) by calling determine_neighbor_fitness(). Derived from Wagner 2023.

  • impute_model : object, optional A custom model object with fit and predict methods used for imputing missing fitness values if any. If None a RandomForestRegressor from scikit-learn is used by default when needed.

  • filter : LandscapeFilter, optional A filter object (inheriting from graphfla.filters.LandscapeFilter) to apply to the constructed landscape to filter certain nodes and edges based on configurations, fitness, or attributes (e.g., edges where both nodes are labeled as non-functional, see Papkou 2023).

  • giant_component : bool, default=True Whether to keep only the largest connected component of the landscape after construction (and filtering if filter is provided).

  • verbose : bool, default=True If provided, overrides the instance's verbosity setting for this method call. Controls printed output during the build process.

Returns

  • None This method modifies the Landscape instance in place, populating its attributes (graph, n_configs, lo, etc.). It does not return a new object.

Warning

calculate_accessibility can be quite computationally expensive for large landscapes (e.g., >300,000 configurations) with many local optima.

.build_from_graph(filepath, verbose=True) -> None Construct a landscape from a saved graph file.

This class method creates a new landscape instance by loading a previously saved graph (in GraphML format, preferably by Landscape.to_graph()). This is significantly faster than building from scratch. It attempts to load essential landscape properties (maximize, epsilon, etc.) stored as graph attributes.

Parameters

  • filepath : str Path to the saved graph file (typically ending in .graphml).
  • verbose : bool, default=True Controls verbosity of output during loading and analysis. If True, prints status messages and warnings during the loading process.

Returns

  • Landscape A new instance of the Landscape (or potentially a subclass if the landscape_class attribute is stored in the graph) populated with the loaded graph and inferred properties.

.to_graph(filepath) -> None Save the landscape graph and essential attributes to a file.

This method serializes the landscape's graph structure and relevant attributes (maximize, epsilon, data_types, class name) to a GraphML file. This file can later be loaded efficiently using the build_from_graph() class method.

Parameters

  • filepath : str The path where the graph file will be saved. If the file doesn't end with .graphml, this extension will be added automatically.

Returns

  • None This method saves the graph to a file and does not return any value.

Notes

  • The GraphML format preserves the graph structure and all vertex/edge attributes.
  • Essential landscape attributes are stored as graph-level attributes for reconstruction.
  • Configurations and related dictionaries are converted to string representations for storage.

.get_data(lo_only=False) -> pandas.DataFrame

Extracts landscape data as a pandas DataFrame.

Returns a DataFrame where rows correspond to configurations (nodes) and columns correspond to their attributes (e.g., fitness, degree, basin information, original features).

Parameters

  • lo_only : bool, default=False
    • If True, returns data only for the configurations identified as local optima (self.lo).
      • If the Local Optima Network (LON) has been computed (self.has_lon is True), relevant attributes from the LON graph (self.lon) are included, prefixed with lon_ (e.g., lon_in_degree, lon_escape_difficulty).
      • If LON is not computed, it returns data from the main graph filtered for local optima nodes.
    • If False (default), returns data for all configurations in the main landscape graph (self.graph), excluding local optima-related attributes (e.g., basin_size_greedy).

Returns

  • pandas.DataFrame A DataFrame containing the attributes of the requested landscape nodes (all or local optima). The index corresponds to the node indices in the original graph. Original feature columns (e.g., 'var_0', 'pos_1') are included if configs was derived from a DataFrame.

.get_lon(mlon=True, min_edge_freq=3, trim=None, **kwargs) -> igraph.Graph

Constructs and returns the Local Optima Network (LON) (Verel 2010).

The LON is a coarse-grained representation of the fitness landscape where nodes are the local optima of the original landscape graph (self.graph), and edges represent the possibility of transitions between their basins of attraction. Edges are typically weighted by the frequency of transitions (escape frequency) between the basins of the connected local optima. This method utilizes the underlying graphfla.landscape.lon.get_lon function for the construction.

Parameters

  • mlon : bool, default=True Whether to construct a Monotonic-LON (M-LON). If True, only edges representing transitions to fitter (or equally fit, depending on the exact implementation of improving edges) local optima are kept in the resulting network. If False, all transitions meeting the min_edge_freq criterion are included.

  • min_edge_freq : int, default=3 The minimum escape frequency required to create an edge between two local optima. The escape frequency from basin A to basin B is the number of 2-mutant neighbors of local optimum A that fall into the basin of local optimum B. An edge is only created if this frequency is greater than min_edge_freq.

  • trim : int, optional, default=None If specified, the LON is trimmed to keep only the top trim outgoing edges from each local optimum node, based on the highest transition frequency (edge weight). If None, all edges meeting the min_edge_freq criterion are kept.

  • *kwargs : Keyword arguments* Additional keyword arguments are passed directly to the underlying graphfla.landscape.lon.get_lon function. Consult its specific documentation if needed, though the main configurable parameters are listed above.

Returns

  • igraph.Graph The constructed Local Optima Network graph. This graph is also stored in the self.lon attribute of the Landscape instance, and the self.has_lon flag is set to True. Nodes in the LON graph have attributes copied from the corresponding local optima in the main landscape graph, plus additional LON-specific attributes like escape_difficulty, improve_rate, and accessibility. Edges have a weight attribute representing the transition frequency.

.describe() -> None

Prints a summary description of the landscape properties.

Provides a quick overview of the landscape's current state, including its size (variables, configurations, connections), the number of identified optima, the optimization direction, and the status of optional analysis calculations (basins, paths, LON).

.determine_local_optima() -> None

Identifies local optima nodes in the landscape graph. It is always called by build_from_data().

Local optima are defined as nodes with no outgoing edges to fitter neighbors. In the context of the directed graph (where edges point towards fitter configurations if maximize=True, or less fit if maximize=False), these are nodes with an out-degree of 0. This method calculates the out-degree for all nodes and identifies those where it is zero. It populates the following attributes:

  • Landscape.graph.vs['is_lo']: A boolean flag indicating if each node is a local optimum.
  • Landscape.n_lo: The total count of local optima found.
  • Landscape.lo: A sorted list of the integer indices of the local optima nodes.

.determine_global_optimum() -> None

Identifies the global optimum node in the landscape graph. It is always called by build_from_data(). The global optimum is the node with the highest fitness value if maximize=True, or the lowest fitness value if maximize=False. It populates the following attribute:

  • go: The integer index of the global optimum node.

.determine_basin_of_attraction() -> None

Calculates the basin of attraction for each node a greedy hill-climbing (a.k.a greedy adaptive walk in biology context, or "best-improvement" hill-climbing in combinatorial optimization, Whitley 2013) algorithm. It is automatically called with build_from_data(calculate_basins=True).

For each node in the landscape graph, this method simulates a greedy hill-climbing. Starting from the node, it repeatedly moves to the neighbor with the largest fitness improvement (or smallest fitness decrease if minimizing) until a local optimum (a node with no improving neighbors) is reached. The starting node is then assigned to the basin of the reached local optimum.

This method calculates and adds the following node attributes to landscape graph:

  • basin_index: The index of the local optimum reached via hill climbing from this node.
  • size_basin_greedy: For local optima nodes, this stores the total number of nodes that converge to the same local optimum as this node via hill climbing.
  • radius_basin_greedy: For local optima nodes, this stores largest the number of steps taken during the hill climb from this node to its corresponding local optimum.

Note

Based on the nature of greedy hill-climbing, the walk trajectory is deterministic, and the measured size of the basins is typically much smaller compared to its stochastic counterparts (e.g., adaptive walks with uniform fixation probability, a.k.a. "first-improvement" hill-climbing).

.determine_accessible_paths() -> None

Determines the size of basins based on accessible paths. It is automatically called with build_from_data(calculate_accessibility=True).

This method calculates an alternative definition of basin size. For each identified local optimum, it finds all nodes in the graph from which that local optimum can be reached by any sequence of fitness-increasing steps (i.e., following the directed edges of the graph). This set of nodes constitutes the "accessible basin" or the set of ancestors. This is the type of basin analyzed in Papkou 2023.

This method calculates and adds the following node attribute to the landscape graph:

  • size_basin_accessible: For local optima nodes, this stores the number of nodes (including the optimum itself) from which the optimum is reachable. For non-optima nodes, this value is typically 0.

Warning

This method can be quite computationally expensive for large landscapes (e.g., >300,000 configurations) with many local optima.

.determine_dist_to_go(distance=None) -> None

Calculates the distance from each node to the global optimum. It is automatically called with build_from_data(calculate_neighbor_fit=True).

This method computes the distance between the configuration of each node and the configuration of the identified global optimum (self.go). The distance metric used depends on the landscape type (e.g., hamming_distance for boolean/sequence, mixed_distance for default) or can be specified explicitly from graphfla.distances.

This method adds the following node attribute to the landscape graph:

  • dist_go: The calculated distance from the node's configuration to the global optimum's configuration.

Parameters

  • distance : callable, optional A distance function from graphfla.distances to use, which calculates the distance between A configuration and ALL configurations in the landscape.

.determine_neighbor_fitness() -> None

Calculates mean neighbor fitness and the difference of this upon single mutation. It is automatically called with build_from_data(calculate_neighbor_fit=True).

This method analyzes the fitness values in the immediate neighborhood of each node. It adds two attributes:

  • mean_neighbor_fit (node attribute): The average fitness value of all neighbors of each node.
  • delta_mean_neighbor_fit (edge attribute): For each directed edge from source node to target node(where target is fitter), this is the difference between the mean neighbor fitness of the target and the source. This can indicate if moving to a fitter neighbor also leads to a neighborhood with higher average fitness, and can be used to determine "evolvability-enhancing mutations" proposed in Wagner 2023, which is implemented in graphfla.analysis.calculate_evol_enhance()).

docs.title: Magic Methods
docs.text:
These methods allow Landscape objects to interact with standard Python operators and functions in intuitive ways (e.g., using len(), indexing [], iteration for...in, etc.).

.__getitem__(index) -> dict

Allows accessing the attributes of a specific configuration (node) using index notation.

You can access a node by its integer index (position in the internal graph structure, typically 0 to n_configs - 1).

Parameters

  • index : int or str The integer index or the string name of the configuration (node) to retrieve.

Returns

  • dict A dictionary containing all attributes associated with the requested node (e.g., {'fitness': 2.5, 'is_lo': False, 'basin_index': 2, ...}).

.__str__() -> str

Returns a human-readable string summary of the landscape.

Provides a concise overview including the class name, number of variables, configurations, connections (edges), and local optima. Called when using print(landscape).

Returns

  • str A summary string describing the landscape.

.__repr__() -> str

Returns a concise string representation of the landscape object.

By default, this calls \_\_str\_\_, providing the same summary information. Useful for inspecting the object in interactive sessions.

Returns

  • str A summary string describing the landscape (same as \_\_str\_\_).

.__len__() -> int

Returns the number of configurations (nodes) in the landscape graph.

Allows using the built-in len() function on the landscape object.

Returns

  • int The total number of nodes (n_configs) in the landscape graph.

.__iter__() -> iterator

Returns an iterator over the configuration indices (node indices) in the landscape.

Allows iterating through the landscape object directly in a for loop, yielding the integer index of each node (from 0 to n_configs - 1).

Returns

  • iterator An iterator yielding integer node indices.

.__contains__(item) -> bool

Checks if a configuration index (or name) exists in the landscape graph.

Allows using the in operator to check for the presence of a node by its integer index or string name (if names are used).

Parameters

  • item : int or str The integer index or string name to check for existence in the graph.

Returns

  • bool It is True if a node with the given index or name exists, False otherwise.

.__bool__() -> bool

Determines the truthiness of the Landscape object.

A Landscape object is considered True if it has been successfully built (via build_from_data or build_from_graph) and its underlying graph contains at least one node. Otherwise, it is considered False. Allows using the landscape directly in boolean contexts like if landscape:.

Returns

  • bool It is True if the landscape is built and non-empty, False otherwise.

Examples

>>> landscape[3]
{
  'p0': 'A', 
  'p1': 'A', 
  'p2': 'A', 
  'p3': 'A', 
  'p4': 'A', 
  'p5': 'A', 
  'p6': 'A', 
  'p7': 'A', 
  'p8': 'C', 
  'fitness': -0.7015213430476732, 
  'in_degree': 14, 
  'out_degree': 9, 
  'pagerank': 2.250598990748081e-06, 
  'is_lo': False, 
  'basin_index': 5071, 
  'size_basin_greedy': 2665, 
  'radius_basin_greedy': 6, 
  'dist_go': 3, 
  'mean_neighbor_fit': -0.6917555252786921
}

>>> print(landscape)
Landscape with 9 variables, 135178 configurations, 1490486 connections, and 514 local optima.

>>> len(landscape)
135178

>>> 3 in landscape
True

>>> for node_index in landscape:
...     # In a real scenario, you would do something with node_index here,
...     # like accessing its data: landscape[node_index]
...     pass
...
