docs.title: ProteinGym Landscape
docs.text: In this page we illustrate how GraphFLA can be used to analyze diverse RNA fitness landscapes.

docs.title: Data Acquisition
docs.text:
The ProteinGym project (Notin et al. 2023) hosts more than 200 deep mutational scanning (DMS) datasets for protein. Each dataset contains a library of protein (amino acid) sequences obtained from mutagenesis experiments and their corresponding fitness values. In total there are more than 2.4M records in ProteinGym.

All datasets can be accessed from GitHub.

docs.title: Landscape Construction
docs.text:
Each sequence-fitness dataset in RNAGym consists of 3 columns: (1) "mutant", (2) "dms_score", and (3) "sequence". To construct RNA fitness landscapes from RNAGym data, we can simply utilize the "sequence" (search space) and "dms_score" (fitness) columns.

Note that in many datasets, the original sequences are often quite long and include genetic backgrounds that remain unmutated across the entire library. While these background regions are important for accurate predictions, they can significantly slow down landscape construction, as identifying neighboring sequences becomes more computationally intensive with longer sequences.

The simple preprocessing script blew can remove common genetic backgrounds in the sequences:

preprocessing

We can then efficiently construct each RNA landscape via:

landscape = ???

docs.title: Large-scale Landscape Analysis
docs.text:
For each of the constructed landscapes, we can apply arbitray landscape analysis features in GraphFLA to decipher its topography. In addition to yield biological insights, such landscape features can also illuminate model prediction performance on these datasets. For example, with landscape features from GraphFLA, we can interrogate questions like:

  1. Why a model performs well on one set of tasks but poorly on another?
  2. Why a model outperforms baseline on task A, but not on task B?

docs.title: References
docs.text: • Pascal Notin et al., “ProteinGym: large-scale benchmarks for protein fitness prediction and design”, NeurIPS (2023).

docs.title: TF Binding Landscapes
docs.text: In this page we showcase the application of GraphFLA to analyze transcription factor binding affinity landscapes (Aguilar-Rodríguez et al. 2017; Payne and Wagner 2014).

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Transcription factors (TFs) are fundamental mediators of gene expression and are involved in numerous evolutionary innovations. Their regulatory effect can be modulated via mutations in TF binding sites, which may alter a TF's affinity for a site and thereby affect gene expression.

The CIS-BP and UniProbe databases provide protein-binding microarray (PBM) data for thousands of TFs across diverse species and DNA-binding domain families. These datasets quantify each TF’s binding affinity—measured by enrichment scores (E-scores)—for all 32,896 possible double-stranded DNA sequences of eight nucleotides in length.

This comprehensive data allows the construction of a combinatorially complete fitness landscape for each TF, capturing its relative binding affinity across all possible binding site variants.

docs.title: Data Acquisition
docs.text:
We can access PBM data for 5,016 TFs from CIS-BP (select "Download Entire Dataset Archive", to save storage, you may only select "E-scores"). This would download a .txt file which contains 32,896 entries and 5,017 columns, where each position describes the binding affinity (E-score) between one particular DNA binding sites (among 32,896 possible variants) and a particular TF (among 5,017 records).

Using this dataset, we can essentially construct 5,016 TF binding landscapes!

docs.title: Landscape Construction
docs.text:
For each landscape, the sequence space is all 32,896 possible variants of 8-mer, double-stranded DNA sequences, and fitness is their corresponding E-score.

fitness = data["???"]

Each landscape can be simply constructed via:

docs.title: Large-scale Landscape Analysis
docs.text: To see generally how navigable are these TF binding landscapes, we can iterate the TFs and construct each landscape and interrogate its navigability via analysis methods in GraphFLA.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• José Aguilar-Rodríguez et al., "A thousand empirical adaptive landscapes and their navigability", Nat Ecol Evol. (2017).
  • Joshua L Payne and Andreas Wagner, "The robustness and evolvability of transcription factor binding sites", Science. (2014).

docs.title: Installation with PyPI
docs.text:
GraphFLA can be easily installed using PyPI:

pip install graphfla

docs.title: Installation from Source
docs.text:
Alternatively, you can install GraphFLA from GitHub source using:

pip install "graphfla @ git+https://github.com/COLA-Laboratory/GraphFLA.git"

Or:

git clone https://github.com/COLA-Laboratory/GraphFLA.git
pip install -e GraphFLA

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: This section gives a brief overview of the GraphFLA API to analyze a simple fitness landscape.

docs.title: Data Loading
docs.text:
We start with a TrpB landscape from Johnston et al. (2024) ("A combinatorially complete epistatic fitness landscape in an enzyme active site").

We first load the data and display some random entries in it: 

import pandas as pd

data = pd.read_csv("data/Johnston2024_TrpB3A.csv")
data.sample(6)

Output 
| sequences | pos1 | pos2 | pos3 | fitness      |
|-----------|------|------|------|--------------|
| AAA       | A    | A    | A    | -0.005517679 |
| RGN       | R    | G    | N    |  0.027979788 |
| SPC       | S    | P    | C    | -0.065551068 |
| RYY       | R    | Y    | Y    | -0.051226487 |
| CDL       | C    | D    | L    | -0.052378826 |
| STE       | S    | T    | E    | -0.058967450 |
| ...       | ...  | ...  | ...  | ...          |

The dataset contains all \(20^3 = 8,000\) possible combinations of amino acids at 3 sites in Tm9D8 and their associated fitness (in terms of catalytic activity). Most ML-ready biological dataset would have the same information (e.g., the benchmark tasks in ProteinGym), though the sequences can be represented in different forms.

docs.title: Date Preparation
docs.text:
To prepare the data for landscape analysis in GraphFLA is exactly the same as that for training an ML model. All we need is to split the dataset into:

  • X: The genotypes. It can either be a list of sequences or a matrix in pd.DataFrame or np.ndarray.
  • y: The fitness values. It can either be a list of fitness values or a pd.Series or np.ndarray.

Let's do this:

X = data["sequences"]
y = data["fitness]

As we mentioned, X can also be a matrix. In this case, each column indicates the amino acid presents at that position.

# this also works as the above one for GraphFLA!
X = data[["pos1", "pos2", "pos3",]]

docs.title: Landscape Construction
docs.text:
Now we can create our landscape! This is much like training an ML model: we first initialize a ProteinLandscape object (since we are dealing wth amino acids sequences), and then build it using our prepared data.

from graphfla.Landscape import ProteinLandscape

landscape = ProteinLandscape(maximize=True)
landscape.build_from_data(X, f, verbose=True)

The maximize parameter tells GraphFLA whether the fitness value is to be maximized (higher the better) or minimized (lower the better). GraphFLA will then run a landscape constrcution pipeline (as you can see in the following output).

Output

landscape.describe()

docs.title: Landscape Analysis
docs.text:
With our constructed landscape, we can apply a rich collection of fitness landscape analysis methods to infer its topography and characteristics, including:

  • ruggedness
  • epistasis
  • navigability
  • neutrality
  • fitness distribution

All analysis methods in GraphFLA have an unified API, and we demonstrate the usage via interrogating the prevalence of different types of epistasis interactions with classify_epistasis().

from graphfla.analysis import classify_epistasis

classify_epistasis(landscape, approximate=True)
Output 
{
    'magnitude epistasis': 0.38521046187197655,
    'sign epistasis': 0.36014372026736785,
    'reciprocal sign epistasis': 0.2546458178606556,
    'positive epistasis': 0.8106778952781974,
    'negative epistasis': 0.18932210472180255
}

Applying this, we find that this TrpB landscape has abundant non-magnitude epistasis, which gives rise to the 62 local peaks as reported in landscape.describe() before. Also, most epistasis are positive, with only 18.9% negative epistasis.

Other analysis methods can be applied in a similar manner, and users can select according to their own analysis goals.
